title: Provisioning Package Scan Incomplete
description: Fires when the provisioning-package scan hit a per-source collection cap (too many .ppkg files, registry packages, or Recovery\Customizations residue files to capture them all). When that happens the allow-list rules (ANALYZE-SEC-005/006) could not evaluate every package, so a non-allow-listed package may have gone unseen. A device with that many provisioning packages is itself unusual and worth a manual look.
explanation:
The provisioning-package scan was incomplete: the device had more provisioning packages than the agent captures per source, so a per-source cap was hit (see `ppkgFilesTruncated` / `packagesTruncated` / `recoveryFilesTruncated` on the `provisioning_package_scan` event).

Because not every package was captured, the allow-list rules (ANALYZE-SEC-005/006) may NOT have evaluated all of them — a non-allow-listed package could be present but unseen. A device carrying this many provisioning packages is also unusual on its own and warrants a manual review.

remediation.title: Review the device manually
steps: Open the provisioning_package_scan event and check which counts exceeded the cap (ppkgFileCount / packageCount vs the *Truncated flags)
Inspect the device directly: C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Provisioning, C:\Windows\Provisioning, C:\Recovery\Customizations, and HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Provisioning\Packages
Confirm whether the large number of provisioning packages is expected for this device/model (e.g. an OEM image) or anomalous
If anomalous, treat it as a potential manipulation and investigate the device's chain of custody
